subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The ability of a screening test to correctly identify individuals who truly have the disease is called:
explanation: Sensitivity is the proportion of true diseased persons correctly labelled positive by the test (true positives / all diseased). Specificity identifies true non-diseased. PPV is the probability that a positive test reflects true disease and depends on prevalence. Reliability refers to consistency/repeatability, not validity.
highYield: Sensitivity = true positives / (true positives + false negatives); detects disease.
pyqNote: Direct repeat; sensitivity vs specificity definitions are perennial.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In a population with very low disease prevalence, a positive result on a screening test will most likely have:
explanation: PPV falls as prevalence falls because most positives become false positives when few people truly have the disease. Sensitivity and specificity are intrinsic test properties and do not change with prevalence. NPV rises in low-prevalence settings.
highYield: Low prevalence -> low PPV, high NPV; sensitivity/specificity are prevalence-independent.
pyqNote: Frequently tested conceptual link between prevalence and PPV.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Which study design is most appropriate to investigate the cause of a sudden outbreak of a rare disease quickly?
explanation: Case-control studies start from outcome (cases) and look backward for exposures, making them fast, cheap and ideal for rare diseases and outbreak investigation. Cohort studies are inefficient for rare outcomes. RCTs are unethical/impractical for studying noxious exposures. Cross-sectional studies measure prevalence, not temporal causation.
highYield: Rare disease or outbreak -> case-control study (starts with disease, looks back).
pyqNote: Classic design-matching MCQ.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Odds ratio is the measure of association typically derived from which study design?
explanation: Case-control studies cannot directly compute incidence, so the odds ratio is used as the estimate of relative risk. Cohort studies and RCTs allow direct calculation of relative risk and attributable risk because incidence is measurable. Ecological studies use group-level data.
highYield: Case-control -> odds ratio; cohort -> relative risk and attributable risk.
pyqNote: Repeatedly asked pairing of design with measure.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In a case-control study, error introduced when cases recall past exposures more thoroughly than controls is called:
explanation: Recall bias is differential accuracy of memory of past exposures between cases and controls, a hallmark limitation of retrospective case-control designs. Selection bias arises from how participants are chosen. Confounding is a third-variable distortion. Lead-time bias relates to screening and apparent survival gain.
highYield: Recall bias is intrinsic to case-control studies (retrospective exposure recall).
pyqNote: Bias identification is a high-frequency item.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Apparent increase in survival time of screen-detected cancer patients caused merely by earlier diagnosis without changing date of death is:
explanation: Lead-time bias is the artefactual lengthening of measured survival because diagnosis is advanced in time while the actual moment of death is unchanged. Length-time bias is preferential detection of slow-growing tumors. Overdiagnosis is detection of disease that would never have caused harm. These are distinct screening pitfalls.
highYield: Lead-time bias = earlier diagnosis only; survival looks longer but death date unchanged.
pyqNote: Tricky screening-bias discrimination tested in recent papers.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Selecting every 10th house in a village list to form a study sample is an example of:
explanation: Systematic sampling selects units at a fixed interval (every kth) after a random start. Simple random uses random numbers/lottery for each unit. Stratified divides the population into strata then samples each. Cluster sampling selects whole groups such as villages.
highYield: Every kth unit with random start = systematic sampling.
pyqNote: Sampling-method identification is a frequent easy point.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Which measure of central tendency is most affected by extreme outlier values?
explanation: The arithmetic mean uses every value, so extreme outliers pull it strongly. The median (positional middle) and mode (most frequent) are resistant to outliers. Range is a measure of dispersion, not central tendency.
highYield: Mean is most influenced by outliers; median is the robust alternative.
pyqNote: Easy biostatistics repeat.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In a normal distribution, what percentage of observations lie within mean plus or minus two standard deviations?
explanation: In a Gaussian curve, mean +/- 1 SD covers about 68%, +/- 2 SD about 95%, and +/- 3 SD about 99.7%. This empirical rule underlies confidence interval construction. The 50% figure corresponds to the median split, not an SD band.
highYield: Normal curve: 1 SD ~68%, 2 SD ~95%, 3 SD ~99.7%.
pyqNote: Empirical rule is repeatedly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Under India's National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme, the recommended first-line regimen for drug-sensitive TB now uses:
explanation: NTEP shifted from intermittent (thrice-weekly) to daily fixed-dose combination regimens dosed by weight bands to improve adherence and reduce resistance. Once-weekly rifapentine is used in latent-TB preventive therapy, not active disease. Streptomycin is no longer first-line for all cases.
highYield: NTEP uses DAILY weight-band FDC regimen for drug-sensitive TB.
pyqNote: Updated NTEP daily-regimen fact is now commonly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The primary diagnostic test recommended for presumptive pulmonary TB under NTEP for upfront use is:
explanation: NTEP recommends upfront molecular nucleic acid amplification testing (CBNAAT/GeneXpert or Truenat) because it rapidly detects Mycobacterium tuberculosis and rifampicin resistance. Smear microscopy misses paucibacillary disease and gives no resistance data. Chest X-ray is supportive, and Mantoux indicates infection, not active disease.
highYield: Upfront NAAT (CBNAAT/Truenat) is the recommended diagnostic in NTEP.
pyqNote: Shift to molecular diagnostics is now a standard exam point.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: India's National TB Elimination Programme set the target year for TB elimination as:
explanation: India aims to eliminate TB by 2025, five years ahead of the global Sustainable Development Goal target of 2030. This accelerated goal is the basis for renaming RNTCP to NTEP. The other years are distractors.
highYield: India TB elimination target = 2025 (SDG global target is 2030).
pyqNote: Frequently asked single-fact item.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Under the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme, the chemoprophylaxis/treatment drug for Plasmodium vivax to prevent relapse is:
explanation: P. vivax forms dormant hepatic hypnozoites that cause relapse; primaquine for 14 days provides radical cure by eliminating them. Chloroquine clears the blood stage but not hypnozoites. Artesunate-based ACT targets P. falciparum blood stages. G6PD status should be checked before primaquine.
highYield: Radical cure of P. vivax relapse = 14-day primaquine (check G6PD).
pyqNote: Vivax radical-cure regimen is a recurring item.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: As per national policy, the first-line treatment for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in India (except north-east states) is:
explanation: India uses artemisinin-based combination therapy; for falciparum the standard ACT is artesunate plus sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (with primaquine single dose as gametocytocidal), except north-eastern states where artemether-lumefantrine is used due to SP resistance. Chloroquine monotherapy is reserved for vivax. Monotherapies promote resistance.
highYield: Falciparum first-line = AS+SP (AL in NE states); add single-dose primaquine.
pyqNote: ACT policy distinction by region is commonly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The Indian national program that targets cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular diseases and stroke through opportunistic screening at health facilities is abbreviated as:
explanation: NPCDCS (National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke) addresses major NCDs through screening, health promotion and NCD clinics. NMHP is the mental health program, RNTCP/NTEP is for TB, and NPCB targets blindness. Population-based NCD screening from age 30 is now integrated under this program.
highYield: NPCDCS = cancer + diabetes + CVD + stroke screening (from age 30).
pyqNote: Program-acronym match frequently appears.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: As per the National Immunization Schedule, BCG, OPV-0 and Hepatitis B birth dose are given at:
explanation: BCG, the zero dose of OPV, and the Hepatitis B birth dose are administered at birth (within the first 24 hours where feasible for Hep B). The 6-, 10- and 14-week visits give pentavalent, OPV, fIPV and PCV/rotavirus. Measles-rubella is at 9 months.
highYield: At birth: BCG + OPV-0 + Hepatitis B birth dose.
pyqNote: Birth-dose vaccines are a classic easy item.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In the Indian Universal Immunization Programme, rotavirus vaccine is scheduled to be given at:
explanation: Rotavirus vaccine in the UIP is given orally at 6, 10 and 14 weeks alongside pentavalent and OPV. It protects against severe rotaviral gastroenteritis, a leading cause of childhood diarrheal deaths. The other timings correspond to measles-rubella and birth-dose vaccines.
highYield: Rotavirus vaccine UIP schedule = 6, 10, 14 weeks (oral).
pyqNote: New-vaccine timing is increasingly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV) under the Indian UIP is administered at:
options: 6, 10 weeks and a booster at 9 months
explanation: PCV in the UIP follows a 2+1 schedule: primary doses at 6 and 10 weeks with a booster at 9 months. It prevents pneumococcal pneumonia and meningitis, major causes of under-five mortality. The other schedules are incorrect for PCV.
highYield: PCV UIP = 6 weeks, 10 weeks, booster at 9 months (2+1).
pyqNote: PCV schedule is a current high-yield update.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The first dose of measles-rubella (MR) vaccine under the National Immunization Schedule is given at:
explanation: The first MR dose is given at 9-12 months and the second at 16-24 months. Earlier administration risks interference from maternal antibodies. The 5-year visit gives DPT and OPV boosters, not the first MR dose.
highYield: MR1 at 9-12 months, MR2 at 16-24 months.
pyqNote: MR timing repeatedly tested after measles-rubella switch.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Infant mortality rate is defined as the number of deaths under one year of age per:
options: 1000 live births in a given year
explanation: IMR = deaths under 1 year per 1000 live births in a year. It is a sensitive indicator of overall health and socioeconomic development. Per 100000 live births is used for maternal mortality ratio, and per 1000 mid-year population is the crude death rate.
highYield: IMR = under-1 deaths per 1000 live births per year.
pyqNote: Indicator definitions are guaranteed marks.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Maternal mortality ratio (MMR) is expressed per:
explanation: MMR is the number of maternal deaths per 100000 live births during a given period. The large denominator reflects the relative rarity of maternal deaths compared with infant deaths. Per 1000 live births is IMR, and per 1000 women aged 15-49 relates to maternal mortality rate, a different measure.
highYield: MMR denominator = per 100000 live births.
pyqNote: MMR vs IMR denominator confusion is frequently exploited.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Total fertility rate represents the average number of children:
options: Born to women in one calendar year
A woman would bear during her entire reproductive period at current age-specific rates
explanation: TFR is the average number of children a woman would have over her lifetime if she experienced the current age-specific fertility rates throughout her reproductive years. It is a summary fertility measure independent of age structure. A TFR around 2.1 represents replacement level.
highYield: TFR ~2.1 = replacement-level fertility; lifetime children per woman.
pyqNote: TFR definition and replacement value commonly asked.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In the demographic cycle, the stage characterized by low birth rate, low death rate and stable, slow-growing or stationary population is the:
explanation: The low stationary stage has both low birth and low death rates, giving a stable or stationary population. The high stationary stage has high birth and high death rates. Early expanding has falling death but high birth rates, while the declining stage has birth rate below death rate causing population decline.
highYield: Low stationary stage = low births + low deaths = stable population.
pyqNote: Demographic-cycle stage discrimination tests deeper recall.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Bitot's spots, a sign used in nutritional surveys, indicate deficiency of:
explanation: Bitot's spots are foamy triangular conjunctival deposits seen in vitamin A deficiency and are a key ocular sign of xerophthalmia. Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets, vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy, and iron deficiency causes anemia. The National Prophylaxis Programme gives vitamin A to prevent these eye changes.
highYield: Bitot's spots = vitamin A deficiency (xerophthalmia).
pyqNote: Classic nutritional-deficiency sign repeat.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In children, the indicator weight-for-height is used primarily to assess:
explanation: Low weight-for-height reflects acute malnutrition, or wasting, indicating recent rapid weight loss. Low height-for-age reflects chronic malnutrition (stunting), and low weight-for-age reflects underweight, a composite. Micronutrient status needs biochemical or clinical assessment.
highYield: Weight-for-height = wasting (acute); height-for-age = stunting (chronic).
pyqNote: Anthropometric-index mapping is repeatedly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Herd immunity is least useful in preventing spread of which of the following?
explanation: Tetanus has no person-to-person transmission because Clostridium tetani spores are acquired from the environment, so immunizing others does not protect the unvaccinated; herd immunity does not operate. Measles, polio and diphtheria spread person-to-person, so high population immunity protects susceptible individuals.
highYield: Herd immunity does NOT apply to tetanus (environmental, non-communicable spread).
pyqNote: Tetanus exception to herd immunity is a favorite trap.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The most reliable indicator that steam sterilization (autoclaving) has been effective is:
explanation: Biological indicators using spores of Geobacillus (Bacillus) stearothermophilus are the gold standard for confirming autoclave efficacy because spore death proves lethal conditions were achieved throughout. Chemical tape and gauges only confirm exposure to heat, not actual microbial kill. Time and temperature alone may not guarantee adequate penetration.
highYield: Autoclave efficacy is confirmed by killing B. stearothermophilus spores (biological indicator).
pyqNote: Sterilization-monitoring item recurs.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The proportion of total incidence in the exposed group attributable to the exposure is best expressed by:
explanation: Attributable risk percent (attributable fraction in the exposed) is the proportion of disease in the exposed group due to the exposure, calculated as (incidence in exposed minus incidence in unexposed) divided by incidence in exposed. Relative risk gives the strength of association. Population attributable risk refers to the whole population, and odds ratio estimates risk in case-control designs.
highYield: Attributable risk % = (Ie - Iu)/Ie; fraction of exposed-group disease due to exposure.
pyqNote: Attributable-risk variants are commonly confused and tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The incubation period concept used to set the duration of quarantine for a contact is based on the:
explanation: Quarantine of contacts lasts for the maximum (longest) incubation period of the disease, ensuring a contact is observed long enough to detect any case that may develop. Using the mean or shortest would release potentially infectious contacts too early. Generation time describes interval between successive cases.
highYield: Quarantine duration = maximum incubation period of the disease.
pyqNote: Quarantine-period logic is a recurring concept.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In the RMNCH+A strategy, the additional 'A' compared with earlier RMNCH frameworks specifically added focus on:
explanation: RMNCH+A adds Adolescent health to Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn and Child Health, recognizing adolescence as a critical window influencing later reproductive and maternal outcomes. Antenatal care and anemia control fall within maternal components but are not the meaning of the added A. The plus-A approach introduced programs like the Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram.
highYield: RMNCH+A: the +A = Adolescent health (linked to RKSK).
pyqNote: Acronym expansion is an easy reliable point.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The scheme providing fixed-day assured, comprehensive and free antenatal care on the 9th of every month to pregnant women is:
explanation: Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA) provides assured, free antenatal checkups on the 9th of every month. Janani Suraksha Yojana is a cash-incentive scheme to promote institutional delivery. Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram entitles free delivery and newborn services. LaQshya focuses on labor-room quality.
highYield: PMSMA = free assured ANC on the 9th of every month.
pyqNote: Maternal-scheme matching frequently appears.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A study reports a relative risk of 1.0 for an exposure-disease relationship. This indicates:
options: No association between exposure and disease
explanation: A relative risk of 1.0 means incidence is equal in exposed and unexposed groups, indicating no association. Values above 1 suggest increased risk and below 1 suggest a protective effect. Statistical association alone, even when present, does not establish causation.
highYield: RR = 1 means no association; >1 risk, <1 protective.
pyqNote: Interpretation of RR values is repeatedly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The most reliable bacteriological indicator of recent fecal contamination of drinking water is:
explanation: E. coli is the best indicator of recent fecal pollution because it is abundant in human feces, does not multiply readily in water, and its presence reliably signals contamination. Total coliforms include environmental organisms. Clostridium perfringens spores indicate remote/intermittent pollution, and fecal streptococci are supplementary indicators.
highYield: E. coli = best indicator of recent fecal contamination of water.
pyqNote: Water-indicator organism is a steady favorite.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The only human disease declared globally eradicated by the World Health Organization is:
explanation: Smallpox is the only human disease ever certified eradicated worldwide (1980). Polio is near elimination but not eradicated. Measles remains widespread, and guinea worm disease (dracunculiasis) is targeted for eradication but not yet achieved. Rinderpest is the only animal disease eradicated.
highYield: Smallpox = only eradicated human disease (1980).
pyqNote: Guaranteed-mark eradication item.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: To compare the mean systolic blood pressure between two independent groups of patients, the most appropriate statistical test is:
explanation: Comparing means of a continuous, normally distributed variable between two independent groups uses the unpaired t-test. The paired t-test is for matched or before-after measurements in the same individuals. The chi-square test compares categorical proportions, and correlation assesses association between two continuous variables.
highYield: Two independent group means (continuous) -> unpaired t-test.
pyqNote: Test-selection items are increasingly common.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Under the National Leprosy Eradication Programme, the duration of multidrug therapy for multibacillary leprosy is:
explanation: Multibacillary leprosy is treated with rifampicin, clofazimine and dapsone for 12 months, whereas paucibacillary leprosy needs 6 months of rifampicin and dapsone. The elimination target of less than 1 case per 10000 population was achieved nationally in 2005, though new cases persist.
highYield: MB leprosy MDT = 12 months; PB leprosy = 6 months.
pyqNote: MDT duration is a recurring program fact.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Caplan's syndrome refers to the coexistence of rheumatoid arthritis with which occupational lung disease?
explanation: Caplan's syndrome is the combination of rheumatoid arthritis with pneumoconiosis, classically coal worker's pneumoconiosis (and also silicosis), producing characteristic rounded lung nodules. Byssinosis results from cotton dust and asbestosis from asbestos, neither defining Caplan's syndrome.
highYield: Caplan's syndrome = rheumatoid arthritis + coal worker's pneumoconiosis (rounded nodules).
pyqNote: Occupational-disease eponym repeatedly tested.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Under the National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme, the recommended iodine content of salt at the consumer level is at least:
explanation: Iodized salt must contain at least 15 ppm of iodine at the consumer level and 30 ppm at the production level to compensate for losses during storage and transport. Adequate iodine prevents goiter, cretinism and other iodine deficiency disorders. The lower values are insufficient distractors.
highYield: Iodine in salt: 15 ppm at consumer level, 30 ppm at production.
pyqNote: IDD salt-iodine values are reliably asked.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: An epidemic curve showing a single sharp peak with all cases occurring within one incubation period suggests:
explanation: A point-source epidemic from a single common exposure produces a sharp single-peaked curve with all cases clustering within one incubation period. Propagated epidemics show successive peaks separated by the incubation period as transmission passes between people. Continuous common-source exposure produces a plateau, and endemic disease maintains a steady baseline.
highYield: Single sharp peak within one incubation period = point-source epidemic.
pyqNote: Epidemic-curve interpretation is a higher-order favorite.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Fractional inactivated polio vaccine (fIPV) is administered by which route under the UIP?
explanation: Fractional IPV is given intradermally (0.1 mL) to conserve vaccine while boosting mucosal and systemic immunity, scheduled at 6 and 14 weeks in the UIP. Oral route is for OPV. Full-dose IPV is intramuscular, but the fractional dose is specifically intradermal.
highYield: fIPV is given intradermally; OPV is oral.
pyqNote: fIPV route is a current high-yield update.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Which vaccine in the UIP is the most sensitive to heat and is kept in the coldest part (freezer) of the cold chain?
explanation: OPV is the most heat-sensitive vaccine and is stored in the deep freezer or coldest compartment; the vaccine vial monitor on OPV guards against heat damage. DPT and Hepatitis B are damaged by freezing and must not be frozen. BCG diluent should not be frozen either.
highYield: OPV is the most heat-sensitive UIP vaccine; DPT/HepB must NOT be frozen.
pyqNote: Cold-chain sensitivity is a recurring detail.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In a typical death due to hanging, the ligature mark is characteristically:
options: Horizontal and below the thyroid cartilage
Oblique, non-continuous and high up above the thyroid cartilage
Horizontal and continuous around the neck
explanation: In hanging the ligature mark is oblique, runs upward toward the knot, is usually incomplete (non-continuous) and lies high in the neck above the thyroid cartilage. In ligature strangulation the mark is typically horizontal, continuous and lower, below the thyroid. These differences are central to distinguishing the two.
highYield: Hanging mark = oblique, high, above thyroid, non-continuous; strangulation = horizontal, low, continuous.
pyqNote: Hanging vs strangulation ligature is a perennial favorite.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A fracture of the hyoid bone or thyroid cartilage is more commonly associated with:
explanation: Fractures of the hyoid bone and thyroid cartilage are more frequent in manual or ligature strangulation because of the direct force applied to the neck, and they are commoner in older individuals whose cartilage has ossified. In hanging such fractures are relatively uncommon, especially in the young. Drowning and smothering do not characteristically cause these fractures.
highYield: Hyoid/thyroid fracture is commoner in strangulation than in hanging.
pyqNote: Hyoid-fracture association is repeatedly tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Tardieu's spots seen in asphyxial deaths are:
options: Areas of lividity on dependent parts
explanation: Tardieu's spots are pinpoint petechial hemorrhages found beneath the pleura and epicardium due to increased intravascular pressure and capillary rupture in asphyxia. They are not blisters, lividity or abrasions. Their presence supports an asphyxial mechanism though they are not entirely specific.
highYield: Tardieu's spots = petechial subpleural/subepicardial hemorrhages in asphyxia.
pyqNote: Tardieu's spots definition is a steady repeat.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Which feature most reliably indicates that a wound was inflicted during life (antemortem)?
options: Vital reaction with hemorrhage into tissues and inflammation
explanation: Antemortem wounds show signs of vital reaction: bleeding into the tissues, clotting, retraction of edges and an inflammatory/healing response, all of which require a living circulation. Postmortem wounds lack significant infiltration of blood and inflammatory change. Gaping and clean margins occur in both and are not reliable.
highYield: Vital reaction (tissue hemorrhage, clotting, inflammation) = antemortem wound.
pyqNote: Antemortem-postmortem distinction is consistently tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Rigor mortis usually first appears in which group of muscles?
options: Small muscles such as eyelids and jaw
explanation: Rigor mortis follows the order of muscle size, appearing first in small muscles such as those of the eyelids, jaw and face, then spreading downward to larger muscles, and disappearing in the same sequence. This pattern is described as proceeding in a head-to-foot direction for onset.
highYield: Rigor mortis starts in small muscles (eyelids/jaw), spreads downward.
pyqNote: Order of rigor mortis is a reliable easy point.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In a temperate climate, rigor mortis is typically fully developed throughout the body by approximately:
explanation: Rigor mortis usually begins within 1-2 hours, takes about 12 hours to involve the whole body, persists for around 12 hours, and then passes off over the next 12 hours (the classic 12-12-12 rule in temperate climates). Onset is faster in hot climates. The figures help estimate time since death.
highYield: Temperate climate rigor: ~12 hours to develop fully (12-12-12 rule).
pyqNote: Rigor timeline numbers are commonly asked.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Postmortem lividity (hypostasis) that does not blanch on pressure indicates that:
options: The blood has coagulated and lividity is fixed
explanation: Lividity initially shifts and blanches on pressure; once it becomes fixed (usually after about 6-8 hours) it no longer blanches because the blood has coagulated and diffused into tissues. Fixation helps estimate time since death and indicates the body's position was unchanged after that point. It is not specific to asphyxia or refrigeration.
highYield: Fixed (non-blanching) lividity = blood coagulated, typically after ~6-8 hours.
pyqNote: Fixation of lividity timing tests deeper recall.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The color of postmortem lividity in carbon monoxide poisoning is characteristically:
explanation: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin to form carboxyhemoglobin, giving lividity a cherry-red color. Hydrogen cyanide also gives a bright/cherry red hue. Brownish (chocolate) discoloration suggests methemoglobinemia from nitrites or potassium chlorate, and bluish lividity occurs in ordinary asphyxia.
highYield: CO poisoning -> cherry-red lividity (carboxyhemoglobin).
pyqNote: Lividity-color clue to poison is repeatedly tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Which IPC section deals with causing death by a rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, often applied in medical negligence and road accidents?
explanation: Section 304A of the Indian Penal Code covers death caused by a rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, and is commonly invoked in motor accidents and allegations of medical negligence. Section 302 is murder, 304B is dowry death, and 376 is rape. Knowing 304A is essential for medicolegal practice.
highYield: IPC 304A = death by rash/negligent act (medical negligence, road accidents).
pyqNote: IPC 304A is among the most frequently asked sections.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Dowry death is dealt with under which section of the Indian Penal Code?
explanation: Section 304B addresses dowry death, defined as the death of a woman by burns or bodily injury, or otherwise than under normal circumstances, within seven years of marriage with evidence of dowry-related cruelty. Section 306 is abetment of suicide and 498A is cruelty by husband or relatives. Section 304A is negligence-related death.
highYield: IPC 304B = dowry death (within 7 years of marriage).
pyqNote: 304B vs 498A distinction is commonly tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 'dying declaration' in India can be validly recorded by a doctor and is admissible primarily under which principle?
options: It must always be recorded by a magistrate to be valid
A person who is about to die is presumed to speak the truth
It is inadmissible without two witnesses
It is valid only if the patient survives
explanation: A dying declaration is admissible on the principle that a dying person is presumed not to lie (nemo moriturus praesumitur mentire). It need not be recorded by a magistrate; a doctor or other competent person may record it, ideally after certifying the patient is conscious and mentally fit. It remains valid even if the declarant later dies, which is in fact the usual situation.
highYield: Dying declaration: based on presumption that a dying person speaks truth; doctor may record it.
pyqNote: Dying-declaration principles are repeatedly examined.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The specific antidote that reactivates inhibited acetylcholinesterase in organophosphorus poisoning is:
explanation: Pralidoxime and other oximes reactivate acetylcholinesterase by removing the phosphate group, provided they are given before enzyme aging occurs. Atropine is used to counter the muscarinic effects but does not reactivate the enzyme. Naloxone reverses opioids and sodium thiosulfate is used in cyanide poisoning.
highYield: OP poisoning: atropine for symptoms, pralidoxime reactivates cholinesterase.
pyqNote: OP antidotes are a guaranteed exam item.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Which clinical sign is most useful to titrate adequate atropinization in organophosphorus poisoning?
options: Drying of secretions and clear chest on auscultation
explanation: Adequate atropinization is judged mainly by drying of bronchial and oral secretions and clearing of the chest, since respiratory secretions are the major cause of death. Pupillary size and heart rate are less reliable endpoints. The aim is to reverse life-threatening muscarinic effects, not to fully dilate pupils.
highYield: Endpoint of atropinization = dry secretions and clear chest.
pyqNote: Atropinization endpoint is a recurring applied item.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The specific antidote for acute paracetamol (acetaminophen) poisoning is:
explanation: N-acetylcysteine replenishes hepatic glutathione and neutralizes the toxic metabolite NAPQI, preventing paracetamol-induced hepatotoxicity, and is most effective within 8-10 hours. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, methylene blue treats methemoglobinemia, and deferoxamine chelates iron. Recognizing antidote-poison pairs is essential in toxicology.
highYield: Paracetamol poisoning antidote = N-acetylcysteine (restores glutathione).
pyqNote: Antidote-matching is a steady source of questions.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A burning metallic taste, garlic odor of breath, and rice-water stools with subsequent peripheral neuropathy suggest poisoning by:
explanation: Acute arsenic poisoning classically causes a metallic taste, garlicky breath odor, severe vomiting and rice-water stools resembling cholera, followed later by peripheral neuropathy and Mees' lines on nails. Lead causes a blue line and wrist drop, mercury causes tremor and stomatitis, and thallium causes alopecia. BAL (dimercaprol) is a key antidote for arsenic.
highYield: Arsenic: garlic breath, rice-water stools, neuropathy, Mees' lines; antidote BAL.
pyqNote: Arsenic features and antidote are perennially tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Dactylography, used in personal identification, is the scientific study of:
explanation: Dactylography is the study of fingerprints, which are unique to each individual and unchanging through life, making them a definitive means of identification. The study of teeth is odontology, of skull/bones is osteology, and of handwriting is questioned-document examination. Fingerprints are individualizing evidence.
highYield: Dactylography = study of fingerprints (unique, unchanging).
pyqNote: Definition-based identification item.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: For definitive identification of severely decomposed or burnt human remains, the most reliable method is:
explanation: DNA profiling provides the most reliable and individualizing identification even when bodies are decomposed, burnt or fragmented, as DNA can be recovered from bone, teeth and tissue. Visual recognition, tattoos and clothing are unreliable when the body is degraded. DNA can be matched to relatives when antemortem samples are unavailable.
highYield: Decomposed/burnt remains -> DNA profiling for definitive identification.
pyqNote: DNA-as-gold-standard identification is commonly tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Which feature is most useful for estimating age in a person around 17-25 years from radiographs?
options: Eruption of the first permanent molar
Fusion of epiphyses of long bones
explanation: Between roughly 17 and 25 years, age is best estimated from the progressive fusion (union) of epiphyses of long bones such as the wrist, elbow and shoulder, which complete in a known sequence. Eruption of the first permanent molar (around 6 years) and deciduous teeth (infancy) are useful only in younger ages. Patellar ossification occurs in early childhood.
highYield: Age 17-25 years estimated by epiphyseal fusion of long bones.
pyqNote: Age-estimation method by age band is a higher-order item.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The total number of deciduous (milk) teeth in a child is:
explanation: There are 20 deciduous teeth, completing eruption by about 2-2.5 years of age, while the permanent dentition has 32 teeth. Knowing eruption timing helps estimate the age of children. The numbers 24 and 28 are distractors not corresponding to either dentition.
highYield: Deciduous teeth = 20; permanent teeth = 32.
pyqNote: Tooth-count fact is reliably asked.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: For a medicolegal examination of an alleged rape survivor who is an adult, valid examination requires:
options: No consent as it is a legal duty
explanation: Examination of an adult survivor of sexual assault requires the informed consent of the survivor herself; the police or court cannot override her autonomy for the physical examination. Treatment and examination of survivors must be provided, but bodily examination still needs consent. For minors, consent of the guardian is required.
highYield: Adult sexual-assault survivor examination needs the survivor's informed consent.
pyqNote: Consent in sexual-assault exams is increasingly tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The legal maxim 'res ipsa loquitur' as applied to medical negligence means:
options: The thing speaks for itself; negligence is self-evident
Expert testimony is unnecessary in all cases
explanation: Res ipsa loquitur means 'the thing speaks for itself': in certain cases the negligence is so obvious (for example a swab left inside the abdomen) that the very occurrence implies negligence without detailed proof. It shifts the explanatory burden to the defendant. It does not abolish the need for evidence in ordinary cases.
highYield: Res ipsa loquitur = the thing speaks for itself; negligence is self-evident.
pyqNote: Negligence maxims are frequently examined.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Privileged communication in medical practice refers to:
options: A doctor's justified disclosure of confidential information to protect a third party or community
explanation: Privileged communication is the disclosure of otherwise confidential patient information to an appropriate authority when it is necessary to protect the interest of the community or a third person, for example informing authorities about an infectious-disease driver. It is a justified exception to confidentiality. It is not about fees, refusal of care or absolute secrecy.
highYield: Privileged communication = justified breach of confidentiality to protect others/community.
pyqNote: Privileged-communication concept is a steady repeat.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The fine, white, lathery froth seen at the mouth and nostrils in fresh-water drowning is mainly due to:
options: Mixing of water, air and respiratory mucus during violent respiratory efforts
explanation: The characteristic fine leathery froth in drowning is formed by churning of water, air and bronchial mucus during the violent respiratory efforts of the drowning process, and it is a vital sign indicating the person was alive when entering the water. It is not due to tuberculosis, decomposition or food aspiration. Similar froth can occur in pulmonary edema.
highYield: Drowning froth = fine leathery froth from water + air + mucus churned by respiration.
pyqNote: Drowning-froth mechanism repeatedly tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The diatom test is used in suspected drowning to:
options: Confirm that drowning occurred in life by detecting diatoms in distant organs
explanation: The diatom test detects microscopic algae (diatoms) in distant organs such as bone marrow, kidney and brain; their presence implies the diatoms reached these organs via active circulation, supporting antemortem drowning. Finding diatoms only in the lungs is less conclusive because they can enter passively. The test does not estimate time of death or detect alcohol.
highYield: Diatoms in distant organs (marrow/kidney) support antemortem drowning.
pyqNote: Diatom-test logic is a higher-order recurring item.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In burns, the line of redness (vital reaction) at the margin between burnt and healthy skin indicates that:
options: The burn was inflicted after death
explanation: A zone of redness or vital reaction (inflammation with hyperemia and blister formation containing protein-rich fluid) at the burn margin proves the burn was sustained during life, as it requires circulation. Postmortem burns lack this vital reaction and the blisters contain little fluid. This distinction is crucial in deciding whether a person was alive when burnt.
highYield: Vital reaction (red line, fluid-rich blisters) at burn margin = antemortem burn.
pyqNote: Antemortem vs postmortem burn is consistently asked.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: According to the rule of nines in an adult, the anterior surface of one entire lower limb represents approximately what percentage of total body surface area?
explanation: By the rule of nines in adults each entire lower limb is 18% of body surface, so the anterior surface of one lower limb is about 9%. The head and neck are 9%, each upper limb 9%, and the trunk front and back 18% each. This estimation guides burn severity and resuscitation.
highYield: Rule of nines: whole lower limb 18%, so anterior half ~9%.
pyqNote: Rule-of-nines calculations appear in forensic and clinical papers.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In a contact gunshot wound of entry over the skull, the wound margins commonly show:
options: Cruciate (stellate) laceration with scorching and soot due to gas expansion
Everted margins typical of an exit wound
explanation: A contact wound over bone-backed areas like the skull produces a stellate or cruciate laceration because expanding gases enter beneath the skin and burst it, with surrounding scorching, soot and muzzle imprint. Distant entry wounds are punched-in with inverted margins, and exit wounds are everted. Recognizing range from wound characteristics is key in firearm cases.
highYield: Contact skull entry wound = stellate/cruciate laceration with soot and scorching.
pyqNote: Range-of-fire wound features are frequently tested.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Which IPC section deals with the offence of rape?
explanation: Section 375 defines rape and Section 376 prescribes its punishment under the Indian Penal Code. Section 302 is murder, 304B is dowry death, and 320 defines grievous hurt. Familiarity with these core sections is essential for medicolegal work.
highYield: IPC 375 defines rape; 376 prescribes punishment.
pyqNote: Section 375/376 is a guaranteed law item.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Ingestion of a strong mineral acid such as sulfuric acid characteristically causes which type of tissue necrosis?
explanation: Strong acids cause coagulative necrosis, forming a hard eschar that limits deep penetration, whereas strong alkalis cause liquefactive (colliquative) necrosis that penetrates deeply and is more dangerous to deeper tissues. Caseous and fat necrosis are unrelated to corrosive ingestion. This acid-alkali distinction is a classic toxicology point.
highYield: Acids -> coagulative necrosis (eschar); alkalis -> liquefactive necrosis (deeper).
pyqNote: Acid vs alkali necrosis is a recurring contrast.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: The earliest external sign of putrefaction in a body lying in air is usually:
options: Greenish discoloration over the right iliac fossa
Skin slippage of the whole body
explanation: The first external sign of putrefaction is greenish discoloration of the skin over the right iliac fossa (caecum), where bacteria-laden bowel contents lie close to the surface. Marbling, bloating and skin slippage appear later as decomposition advances. This sequence aids estimation of the postmortem interval.
highYield: First putrefaction sign = green discoloration over right iliac fossa (caecum).
pyqNote: Earliest-putrefaction-sign detail is a recurring higher-order item.
